_meta.annotator_view: pass A sheets show {target, text}; pass B sheets show {condition set (by condition_set_id), bindings, text} and omit target/concept word - see annotation-protocol.md
_meta.presentation_order: file order = presentation order for both annotation passes (concept blocks and within-block order deterministically shuffled under kot-g3-mat/1|seed=0)
_meta.status: CANDIDATE-MATERIALS: stimuli only, no gold labels anywhere in this file

text: A hermit kept a daily weather diary in his mountain hut for decades. He was found one autumn, long gone from old age; the diary's last pages had been eaten by damp and mice, and no one alive had seen him in twenty years. That his diary-keeping stopped at some point is certain; no one can know when, and no one even knew it was happening.

At noon the fire alarm cleared the conference center; the in-person sessions stopped and everyone was sent out. Within twenty minutes the organizers had moved every remaining talk onto the video platform, and the conference ran online through the final keynote at six.

The sitcom's final episode aired in May 2012; the studio struck the sets, the cast dispersed, and for years nothing more of it existed to watch than reruns. In 2023 the same cast and writers returned, and new episodes have aired weekly since.

At 8:41 the slow movement's last note faded and the hall went silent - forty seconds of stillness, only a cough, while the conductor held his hands raised. Then the third movement burst out, and the performance carried on till nine.

At 4:05 p.m., with the score level, lightning cracked over the stadium and the referee sent both teams inside; the pitch sat empty under rain all evening. At 10 a.m. the next morning the same match resumed at the same score and ran to a finish by noon.
The referee looked at his watch and blew three sharp blasts at 4:47 p.m.; players shook hands and the stands started to empty. The match had run since three o'clock with only the half-time pause.

The chamber orchestra's final chord faded at 10:58 p.m., the conductor lowered his arms, and the hall lights came up; by 11:15 the musicians were packing. Anyone passing earlier that evening would have heard the music going since eight.
At 3:30 sharp the chairwoman summarized the action items, thanked everyone, and closed her folder; people unplugged laptops and drifted out. The room had been in session since one.

No one can put a date on it, but at some point Rafael and Bruno's bond of thirty years was simply over: the calls had spaced out, the visits stopped, a last text sat unanswered somewhere in 2019 or 2020. Neither can name a day, a quarrel, or a final anything.

Rain had hammered the valley since midnight, but at first light it thinned, and by 6:20 a.m. the last drops had stopped; the gauges recorded nothing after that. Farmers who had watched it all night went to bed.

target: The feud began at the 2011 wedding toast.
text: Looking back, both families date the feud to the wedding toast of 2011 - the joke that landed wrong. Nothing visible followed for months: the same dinners, the same holidays. Only by the next summer had invitations quietly stopped in both directions.

target: The town's electricity supply began at 6:14 p.m.
text: The line crews worked all night after the ice storm. At 6:14 p.m. the substation was re-energized and the whole district lit up - kettles, radiators, streetlamps - and stayed lit through the winter evening. The town had been dark for thirty hours.

The starter's pistol fired at 2:00:00 and the eight sprinters exploded off the blocks - for four seconds, until the recall gun cracked: a lane infringement. Everyone walked back; the re-run went off at 2:12.

target: The match began at 10:00 a.m. Sunday.
text: Rain had stopped Saturday's match at 4:05 with the score level; the teams came back Sunday at 10:00 a.m., the referee whistled, and the same match played on from the same score to a finish at noon.
The house lights dimmed at exactly 8:00 p.m., the projector rolled the studio logo, and the film ran from its first frame; two hours of it lay ahead. At 7:59 the screen had still been blank.

At 3:07:12 the lab's high-speed camera caught the single spark-gap discharge: nothing, then the flash - one frame bright, measurable in microseconds - then nothing again. The team logged the event's clock time to the millisecond.

target: Hana's wrist pain began in the spring.
text: Asked when the ache in her wrist started, Hana can only shrug: some week in the spring it was not there, some week later it was - first only after long days, then most evenings, now constantly. There was never a first morning she could point to.
The pavement was dry all morning. At 12:04, fat drops started spotting the square, and within a minute rain was falling steadily; it kept up all afternoon.
Professor Silva walked in at 9:05, set down her notes, and spoke her first sentence; the lecture ran fifty minutes from that word. Before it, only chatter.
On Monday at 7 a.m. the first excavator bit into the cleared lot - the project's first physical work after months of empty fencing - and crews have worked the site daily since.

target: Dan believes the plant will close.
text: A supplier's rep told Dan over coffee that the plant across town will shut by winter. Dan has no way to check it and has not tried, but he has been repeating it as fact all week and has started quietly asking around about openings elsewhere.

Vera derived the result herself, re-checked it two independent ways, and had it confirmed by the journal's referee: the sum is 512, and she knows it as surely as anything in her field. Ask her whether the sum is 512 and she will say: 'Of course - it is.'

Nothing in Sam's forty years has brought Antarctica to mind more than a handful of times. Asked out of nowhere at a quiz night whether it is cold there, he answers instantly and correctly, as anyone would - though the thought had not crossed his mind in years.

target: Petra believes it will rain tomorrow.
text: The forecast puts seventy percent on rain tomorrow, and Petra plans accordingly: umbrella by the door, market trip moved to Thursday. Pressed, she would say it will probably rain - she would not put it stronger than that.

target: Imke believes the brother betrayed the family.
text: Deep in drafting her novel's third act, Imke spends the day inside the thought 'the brother betrayed the family' - turning it over, feeling its weight, writing from within it. It is her invention; there is no brother. By dinner she is talking about him like a real person.

target: The warden believes the building is empty.
text: Running the evacuation drill, the fire warden proceeds on the standing assumption that every office is empty once the sweep team radios clear - he treats it as true and acts on it, while knowing full well no sweep is infallible. Privately he would tell you: you never really know.

target: Sofia believes the neighbor's meteorite story.
text: The neighbor swears a meteorite came down in his beet field in the eighties and shows a fist-sized black stone on his mantel. Sofia finds the story convincing - the stone looks the part - though she is aware no one ever tested it and she has only his word.

target: Greta believes the pellets relieve her headaches.
text: Whenever a headache starts, Greta takes the little white pellets from the homeopath's vial and is sure they take the edge off within the hour. She has read the skeptical articles her son sends and knows the trials are against her; still, she restocks the vial every month.

target: The juror believes the defendant is innocent.
text: Juror number six follows the judge's instruction to the letter: throughout the trial she reasons from 'the defendant is innocent' as her fixed starting point, and she obviously does not know whether it is true - that is what the trial is for. Asked afterward, she said she had kept an open mind the whole time.

target: Wanda believes her team will win tomorrow.
text: Ahead of the derby, Wanda has told everyone who will listen that her club takes it tomorrow - she can feel it. She knows no result in football is knowable in advance; she has put a tenner on it anyway and sleeps easy.

target: Tunde is a friend of Espen.
text: What started as adjacent desks became Saturday hikes, then holidays with both families. After eight years, Tunde plans his weekends around what he and Espen might do, wants Espen's promotion to come through almost more than his own, and could not bear bad news reaching him.

target: Ricardo is a friend of Selma.
text: Ricardo has poured Selma's flat white most mornings for three years. He knows her order, her dog's name, her mother's hip trouble; the ten-minute chat when she comes in is a bright spot of his shift, he hopes her mother mends, and he would hate for anything to go wrong for her. Outside the cafe they have never exchanged a word; neither knows where the other lives.

target: Lotte is a friend of Sacha.
text: Pen pals since a school exchange at eleven, Lotte and Sacha have met dozens of times since, across two countries. Lotte saves things up all week to tell her, delights in every visit, wishes her nothing but good, and would drop everything if trouble came her way.

target: Dana is a friend of Mei.
text: Roommates in college, Dana and Mei have kept it up for a decade since: monthly calls that run two hours, the standing December visit, the airport pickups no one else would do. Dana lights up when plans with Mei land on the calendar, wants every good thing for her, and grieved with her through the divorce like it was her own.

target: Marek is a friend of the senior partner.
text: Marek, two years into the firm, is assigned to the senior partner's team. He relishes every working session with her, wants her cases to go well, and would be genuinely sorry to see anything hurt her standing. She, for her part, has twice needed prompting for his name and thinks of him - when at all - as 'the new analyst'.

target: Marisol is a friend of Beatriz.
text: Since Marisol moved to the other coast nine years ago, she and Beatriz have done nothing together - no visits, no calls to speak of, a birthday message most years. Yet Marisol names her without hesitation among the people she is closest to; some bonds, she says, do not need upkeep, and if Beatriz called at 3 a.m. she would answer.

target: Ines is a friend of Vera.
text: Ines is not speaking to Vera this week - not since Vera let slip the surprise party. Right now Ines rather hopes Vera stews in guilt over it a few days more; she has said so. Nobody who knows them doubts where the two of them will be next month: side by side at the market as they have been every Saturday for twenty years.

target: Nikos is a friend of Berta.
text: Old Nikos and Berta have gardened over the fence for fifteen years - seedlings traded in spring, gluts of tomatoes in August, a check-in knock whenever the other's curtains stay drawn past nine. He looks forward to her company all winter, hopes only good for her, and would hate to see her hurt.

target: Henrik is a friend of Olaf.
text: Since Olaf's diagnosis, the Tuesday visits have become the heaviest hours of Henrik's week: the hospital light, the searching for words, the drive home in silence. Nothing about the visits feels good and he slightly dreads them - and he has not missed one in eight months, and will not.

target: Rosa is a friend of her grandson.
text: Rosa's grandson comes up the hill every Sunday, and it is the best of her week: the cooking together, the cards after lunch, his stories from town. She wants his whole life to go well and would take any hurt of his onto herself if she could. When the neighbor called him her 'young companion', Rosa laughed: he is family - that is a different thing.

target: Lucia's reference line is a bookmark.
text: Finishing her thesis chapter, Lucia added a numbered reference: author, title, journal, volume, and pages for a 1998 survey she relies on. Whenever she needs to re-check the survey, the reference line tells her exactly where in the library's holdings it sits.

target: Emil's leather strip is a bookmark.
text: Grandpa Emil keeps a thin pressed-leather strip, plain and unmarked, that he lays between the pages of whatever novel he is reading before he sleeps. Last night he left it at the start of chapter nine of a detective story, where it waits for him tonight.

target: The saved-pages entry is a bookmark.
text: In her e-reader, Joana pressed and held page 214 of the novel and chose 'save this page'. The reader added the page to her saved-pages list, showing the chapter name and page number, so she can jump back to that page any evening.

target: Ben's catalog record is a bookmark.
text: Cataloguer Ben typed a record for a newly acquired atlas: title, author, year, and the shelf mark 'G-114.7'. The record he typed is in the library system; looking it up tells him - and anyone - precisely which shelf the atlas stands on.

target: The entry Rosa's app created is a bookmark.
text: Rosa listens to a history podcast on her phone. At minute 42 of an episode she tapped 'save position'; the app created an entry with the episode title and timestamp so she can return to that exact spot on her commute.

Before her trip, Dana wrote on a card for her own future self: 'Spare key - under the blue flowerpot by the back door', and tucked the card in her wallet. Months later, locked out, she read it and went straight to the flowerpot.

bindings.this something: the saved item in the 'Moving' folder

target: The saved item in the 'Moving' folder is a bookmark.
text: Researching visa rules, Tomas saved the embassy's requirements page with his browser's star button into a folder he named 'Moving'. The saved item shows the page title and address; two months later he opened it from that folder to re-check a detail.

bindings.this something: the entry in Selma's saved list

target: The entry in Selma's saved list is a bookmark.
text: Years ago Selma starred a fan site about a canceled TV show; the entry with the site's name and address still sits in her browser's saved list. The site was taken down in 2021: the address now leads nowhere, and nothing anyone can do with the entry will show where - or whether - the pages exist anymore.

bindings.this something: the entry in Priya's saved list

target: The entry in Priya's saved list is a bookmark.
text: Halfway through a long article on sourdough starters, Priya clicked the star icon in her browser. The entry it created - the article's title and web address - now sits in her saved list, and with it she can get back to the article whenever she wants.

bindings.this something: the entry on the 'frequently visited' rail

target: The entry on the 'frequently visited' rail is a bookmark.
text: Nobody in the office set it up: the team's browser ships with a 'frequently visited' rail that, by itself, added an entry for the payroll portal - title and address - after the accountants kept opening it. The accountants now click that entry every payday.

Aldo's laptop hinge snapped at one corner last month - you can see the cracked plastic, and the lid now flops past flat if you tip it back too far. He props it against a stack of books at his desk, where it runs his email, his spreadsheets, and his video calls exactly as it always has, eight hours a day.

The zipper on Vera's winter coat has been getting stiffer for two seasons. Nothing ever happened to it - no snag, no yank, no moment anyone could name - but this morning the slider simply would not move in either direction, and the coat cannot be closed at all.

Mid-dinner there was a crack from under Uncle Beno's chair: one back leg had snapped clean through at the joint. The chair now leans in the corner; anyone who sat on it would go straight to the floor.

The movers dropped the washing machine on the stairs. Since then the drum has a visible crack and the tub leaks onto the floor within a minute of starting any cycle; running it at all just floods the laundry room.

At the scrapyard, the old sedan went through the crusher Monday and is now a neat half-meter cube of folded metal in row twelve. Everything about the car came apart or folded in the process, and of course nothing car-like can be done with it anymore.

Behind the cabin, Georg took the fallen birch limb, snapped it over his knee into a dozen short lengths, and stacked them in the basket by the stove. As a limb it is finished - nobody could use it now for anything a branch is for.

Rui's phone slipped off the balcony rail and hit the tiles face-down. The glass is a spiderweb now and the screen shows nothing; taps and buttons do nothing at all. It sits in a drawer while he uses his old handset.

A gust slammed Nils's kite into the sea wall and the main spar snapped in the middle. The sail flaps around the two loose halves; it cannot hold shape, and no amount of string will get it into the air today.

Since Tuesday's update, the banking app opens to a white screen and goes no further on any phone in the family; the bank's own status page says as much. Nothing was dropped, struck, or snapped anywhere - a bad line of code went out - yet no customer can log in.

The old grain silo came down on schedule Sunday: the demolition crew's charges buckled its legs and forty meters of concrete came apart onto the gravel in seconds, exactly as engineered. What stands there now is a fenced heap of rubble awaiting trucks; nothing about the silo can be used for anything.

target: The baker gave the loaf to the volunteer.
text: At closing, the baker boxed the last loaf and handed it across the counter to the shelter volunteer, waving away her coins. The bread went into the shelter's van with the rest of the evening's donations.

target: Marisa gave the man her seat.
text: The tram was packed. Seeing the old man sway with his cane, Marisa stood and motioned him into her seat, taking the rail herself for the remaining six stops.

target: Hedda gave the cottage to Karin.
text: Aunt Hedda's will, written years before her passing, left the lakeside cottage to her niece Karin. Probate closed in March, eight months after Hedda's funeral, and the deed went into Karin's name; Karin summers there now.

target: The collector gave the painting to the museum.
text: The collector signed the deed of donation at the museum on Tuesday, and the painting left his wall for their gallery the same week. It hangs with a card naming his collection; it is the museum's now, outright, and he sought nothing in return.

target: Marta gave the car to Jonas.
text: Marta listed her hatchback, and Jonas came Saturday with 3,000 in cash. She counted the notes, signed over the registration, and dropped the keys in his palm; he drove it home. Before Saturday the car was hers alone; now it is his alone.

target: Ana gave the bicycle to her cousin.
text: Ana wheeled her old bicycle over to her cousin's on Saturday: 'It's yours now,' she said, handing over the lock keys too. Her cousin rides it to school; Ana kept nothing and expects nothing back for it.

target: Grandfather gave the watch to Malik.
text: At the graduation dinner, Grandfather unstrapped the watch he had worn for fifty years and closed Malik's hand around it. 'Yours,' he said. Malik wears it daily; the old man smiles at his bare wrist.

target: The courier gave Ravi the parcel.
text: The courier rang, confirmed Ravi's name, and handed him the parcel from the depot; Ravi signed the pad and took it inside. The contents were a jacket Ravi had ordered and paid for himself.

target: Emil gave the knife to the airport authority.
text: At the security line, the officer pointed at Emil's pocketknife: it goes in the bin or he does not fly. Emil placed it on the tray and watched it disappear into the confiscation drawer. The airport disposes of such items in bulk; it was Emil's knife, and now, as the sign says, such items become property of the authority.

target: Ilya gave the album to Petra.
text: At the collectors' fair, Ilya and Petra swapped outright: his complete 1936 stamp album for her case of silver denarii, each item examined, each pronounced fair. His album sits on her shelf now and the coins in his cabinet; neither can claim what they carried in.
Kenji centered the clay, drew it up between wet palms, collared the neck, and cut the finished bowl off the wheel with a wire - a bowl now, where twenty minutes before there had been a lump of clay.

Six-year-old Bo folded a sheet of newspaper corner over corner the way his grandmother showed him, creased the hull, tugged out the sails - and there it was, a paper boat where a page of yesterday's news had been.
After four hours of deadlock, the board voted 7-2 at 3 p.m. and the matter was settled: the factory will close in June. The chair read the resolution into the minutes.

Over three weekends Tomas milled, joined, and finished a dining table from rough oak planks in his garage shop. Where there had been a stack of lumber in May, the finished table went into the kitchen in June.
Slamming the front door on his way out, Viktor felt the wall shudder; a hairline crack now runs half a meter up the plaster above the frame, where the surface was smooth this morning.

No one planned the shortcut: years of students cutting the corner between the library and the dining hall wore the grass to dirt, and the dirt to a smooth trodden line. Groundskeepers stopped reseeding it and finally paved the route everyone's feet had drawn.
At 4 a.m. Rosa mixed, proofed, shaped, and baked the day's first batch: forty rolls golden on the racks by six. At 3 a.m. there had been flour, water, salt, and yeast.
Over two million years the river cut down through the plateau's sandstone, carrying the canyon deeper grain by grain; today it runs four hundred meters below the rim it once flowed level with.
Farmer Anselm planted the seed in May, watered, weeded, and fed the vine all summer, and cut the four-kilo pumpkin from it in October - a pumpkin there would never have been had he done none of it.
Zoya chose the stone, sketched what she wanted, paid the deposit, and six weeks later collected her ring from the goldsmith's bench - a ring that exists only because she walked in and ordered it.

target: The speaker's noon announcement is a reminder.
text: Old Mr. Lund's daughter worries he will skip meals, so he told his smart speaker to say 'time for lunch' at noon every day. He is nearly blind; there is nothing to look at - at noon the kitchen simply says it, lunch comes to mind, and he eats.

Every evening Farah writes a page about the day in a notebook she keeps for the purpose, because she wants to be able to think about these years later in life. Some Sundays she reads old pages; the days return to her as she reads, and little of that time is gone from her.

target: The calendar alert Amir set is a reminder.
text: Amir added an entry to his calendar for his mother's birthday with an alert two days before. When the alert appeared on his screen, the birthday came to mind, he ordered flowers, and the day did not slip past him as it had the year before.

bindings.this something: the 8 a.m. card on the tablet

target: The 8 a.m. card on Mr. Petrov's tablet is a reminder.
text: The ward nurse set up Mr. Petrov's tablet so that at 8 a.m. daily a card appears saying 'blood-pressure tablet, with food'. Mr. Petrov, who asked for nothing, sees the card each morning, thinks of his medicine, and takes it. The nurse set it up because his chart requires it.

target: The framed photograph is a reminder.
text: Tomas framed a photograph from their wedding day and set it on the hallway shelf, wanting the day to stay present to them both as the years pass. He sees it every morning leaving for work; the day comes back to him each time, vivid as ever.

On the last day in Lisbon, Chloe bought a small painted tile and hung it by her desk, wanting to be able to think back on that trip in the years ahead. Whenever her eye falls on it, the trams and the bakery on the corner come back to her; the trip has never faded from her memory.

bindings.this something: the red string on Bruno's finger

target: Bruno's red string is a reminder.
text: Worried the dentist appointment would slip his mind, Bruno tied a red string around his left index finger at breakfast. Every time it caught his eye during the day, the appointment came back to him, and at four o'clock he was in the waiting room.

Greta wrote 'passport renewal - window closes the 14th!' on a card and propped it by the door, wanting it on her mind that week. The card slid behind the umbrella stand the same evening; she never saw it again, the 14th passed, and the matter went entirely out of her head until the travel agent asked.

target: Yuki's sticky note is a reminder.
text: Yuki wrote 'CALL THE PLUMBER - MONDAY' on a sticky note and put it at eye level on the fridge, because she wanted the call on her mind come Monday. Monday morning she saw it while making coffee, thought of the leak, and called.

bindings.this something: the 9 p.m. entry on Ester's phone

target: The 9 p.m. entry on Ester's phone is a reminder.
text: Before bed, Ester sets her phone to sound at 9 p.m. with the label 'evening pills'. Each night the banner appears, she sees it, thinks of the pills, and takes them; since she started this she has not once missed a dose.

Through the flat's cold January, the little oil heater by Duna's desk kept her corner at a livable temperature. She does not do anything with it, exactly - it just runs - but her hands stayed warm enough to work, and the damp never got into that room.

Ana's multi-tool rides in her pack all season: this week alone she has tightened a stove screw with it, sliced cord for a tarp line, pulled a splinter with its tweezers, and opened two cans. Whatever the campsite throws at her, it usually turns out the little tool can handle it.

The new chef's knife holds its edge: Fatou preps a whole service with it - chickens jointed, onions diced fine, herbs cut to ribbons, squash halved - one knife, everything the menu needs, twice as fast as the old blade.

Since Marisol put the folding step-stool in the kitchen, the top shelves are hers again: she gets down the stockpot, changes the ceiling bulb, reaches the good vases, dusts the cabinet tops. It has turned a dozen small jobs easy that used to mean waiting for her tall son to visit.

Iker couriers packages across the city, and the routing app on his handlebars is how he does it: it threads him around closures, locates addresses in the maze of the old town, reorders his drops when jobs come in, and gets him home before dark with more deliveries done.

The little chrome opener lives on Beni's keyring and does exactly one thing: it lifts bottle caps. It has done so maybe once a month for years - at picnics, at the beach, when the drawer one is missing - and that is the whole of its career.

Whatever the studio needs, Marek can do: he rewires lights, talks down difficult clients, patches the booking system, covers shifts without complaint. Because he is around, the owner has taken on jobs she could never have run alone.

target: Leyla's bond with the farmer is useful.
text: Leyla's oldest bond, with a farmer up the valley, has carried her family through: hay when her barn flooded, a truck each market day, advice that saved her orchard, a co-signature at the bank. Because she has this bond, half of what she has built was possible.

In the safe lies the gold coin Selim's grandmother left him - a krugerrand, worth a good deal more every year. If he ever wants to fund a workshop, cover an emergency, or put down a deposit, selling it would open each of those doors. It sits in the dark, appreciating.

The fire extinguisher has hung by Greta's stove for eleven years, unused. It exists for a single job that has never yet arisen; if flames ever do jump from a pan, it will be the one thing in the kitchen that matters. She checks its gauge each spring and rehangs it.

bindings.this something: what happened at 11:52 p.m. on the ring road

target: What happened at 11:52 p.m. on the ring road is a birth.
text: The taxi never made it to the hospital. On the shoulder of the ring road at 11:52 p.m., with the driver relaying instructions from an emergency dispatcher, Amara delivered her son. Mother and child were checked at the hospital an hour later and both were fine.

bindings.this something: the event recorded in the parish book

target: The event recorded in the parish book is a birth.
text: The composer's biography opens with a parish record from 1756: on the morning of January 27, in a small apartment on Getreidegasse, his mother delivered him after an uneventful labor, and the family entered the event in the parish book that week.

bindings.this something: what happened at 6:40 a.m. in the back bedroom

target: What happened at 6:40 a.m. in the back bedroom is a birth.
text: The farm sits far from town, so when the midwife arrived at dawn, Leena's labor was already advanced. In the back bedroom at 6:40 a.m. she delivered a boy, who cried at once. The district nurse recorded the event when she visited that afternoon.

bindings.this something: what happened at 3:40 p.m. in the operating theater

target: What happened at 3:40 p.m. in the operating theater is a birth.
text: For the aortic operation, the surgical team deliberately stopped Mr. Halim's heart and cooled his body; for fifty minutes there was no heartbeat and no brain activity - by the monitors, no living patient. At 3:40 p.m. the team restarted his heart. He woke that evening and recovered fully.

bindings.this something: what happened at 10:03 a.m. in the lab

target: What happened at 10:03 a.m. in the lab is a birth.
text: In the fertility clinic's lab at 10:03 a.m., an embryologist watched the recording of a fertilization completing: before that moment there was an egg cell and a sperm cell and no individual anyone could point to; from that moment there was a single living embryo, which the clinic logged with an ID and which was implanted and carried to term. The daughter, now six, is that same individual.

bindings.this something: what happened at 4:17 p.m. on the beach

target: What happened at 4:17 p.m. on the beach is a birth.
text: Lifeguards pulled Viktor from the lake without pulse or breathing; a doctor on the beach could detect no heartbeat for over four minutes. At 4:17 p.m. the defibrillator restored his heartbeat and he gasped. Before 4:17 there was, clinically, no living patient on the sand; after it, Viktor was alive and was talking within the hour.

bindings.this something: what happened at 2:05 p.m. in the delivery room

target: What happened at 2:05 p.m. is a birth.
text: Dr. Osei reviews the delivery-room video with her students. On it, at 2:05 p.m., a full-term boy emerges and takes his first breath. She points out that on the fetal monitor's record, the same boy had a steady heartbeat, was moving, and was hiccupping in the womb for months before that afternoon - a living patient she had been tracking since spring.

target: What happened at 9:12 a.m. is a birth.
text: At thirty-nine weeks, monitoring could detect no heartbeat, and the doctors told Sofia her baby had not survived. The next morning she went through labor, and at 9:12 a.m. she delivered her son, silent and still. The ward's paperwork records the event at 9:12, on the form it keeps for such cases.

bindings.this something: what happened in the delivery room that night

target: What happened in the delivery room that night is a birth.
text: Nadia's labor stretched across two calendar days. Her daughter's head emerged at 11:48 p.m. on Tuesday, the delivery was only complete at 12:26 a.m. Wednesday, and the cord was cut at 12:31. Filling in the registry form's single 'time of event' box, the exhausted registrar hesitated over which minute to write.

bindings.this something: what happened at 3:14 a.m. in the delivery ward

target: What happened at 3:14 a.m. is a birth.
text: At 3:14 a.m. in the county hospital's delivery ward, after six hours of labor, Ines delivered a healthy girl. The midwife noted the time on the chart, and the ward clerk entered the event into the registry the same morning.

bindings.this something: what happened in Vala's room that morning

target: What happened in Vala's room that morning is a death.
text: Grandmother Vala slipped away in her sleep sometime before six, when the carer came in with tea and found her still, without breath or pulse. The doctor who came at seven confirmed it and filled in the certificate.

target: What happened at 5:03 p.m. is a death.
text: On the highway shoulder, paramedics worked on the motorcyclist for twenty minutes, but at 5:03 p.m. the monitor showed a flat line and the lead medic called it, noting the time for the coroner. He was twenty-six.

bindings.this something: what happened to the old oak

target: What happened to the old oak is a death.
text: The four-hundred-year-old oak on the green had stood since the town's founding. After the beetles got under its bark, the arborist watched it fail across two summers; this spring no leaf opened on it, and he recorded the tree as gone. The parish newsletter mourned it by name.

target: What happened at 2:40 a.m. is a death.
text: Mr. Adeyemi, ninety-one, had been fading for days in the hospice. At 2:40 a.m. his breathing stopped and did not resume; the night nurse recorded the time and drew the curtain. The family was told at dawn.

bindings.this something: what happened at 8:51 p.m. on the ring road

target: What happened at 8:51 p.m. is a death.
text: At 8:51 p.m. a lorry crossed the median on the ring road and struck a car head-on; the car's driver was killed instantly. The police report describes what happened at 8:51 - the crossing, the impact, the aftermath - in four dense pages.

bindings.this something: what happened at 14:07 in Hall C

target: What happened at 14:07 in Hall C is a death.
text: The inquiry fixed it precisely: at 14:07 the pressure vessel in Hall C ruptured, and the fitter standing beside it was killed at once. The plant's incident log for 14:07 runs to a single line; the union's plaque by the gate names the man and the minute.

bindings.this something: what happened at 10:22 in the square

target: What happened at 10:22 in the square is a death.
text: At dawn in 1793, before a crowd in the square, the sentence was carried out on the deposed king: the blade fell at 10:22 by the clock tower, and he was gone. Histories give the event in the square its own name and hour.

bindings.this something: what happened to Mrs. Okafor that week

target: What happened to Mrs. Okafor that week is a death.
text: Mrs. Okafor's family kept vigil for two days as her organs failed one by one: by Tuesday evening her kidneys had stopped; through Wednesday her breathing thinned; the monitors were switched off Thursday at noon. No single moment could be pointed to, yet the certificate's 'time' box required one, and the registrar wrote 'Thursday 12:00' with a shrug.

bindings.this something: the event recorded for that December night

target: The event recorded for that December night is a death.
text: The court archive holds the physician's certificate from December 1791: in the small apartment on Rauhensteingasse, shortly before one o'clock in the morning, the composer's illness took him; the household register records the event that same night.

target: What happened at 10:22 a.m. is a death.
text: In the cath lab, Mr. Reyes's heart stopped outright at 10:22 a.m. - no pulse, no breathing, monitors flat for six minutes while the team worked. At 10:28 his heartbeat was restored; he left the hospital nine days later and now tells everyone about that morning.

When Priya learned her closest confidante had shared her private news around the office, she thought about it constantly: that it was a wrong thing, that she wished it undone, that she needed to ask why. There was a weight on her chest for days. Asked about it, she shook her head - no heat in her voice at all - and said only that she felt wounded, and wanted to understand.

A leaked draft shows the council will vote next week on flattening the playground for parking. Nothing is decided and nothing torn down - the swings stand right there - but reading the draft Idris felt his face go hot; he was already pacing, composing objections, listing neighbors to call tonight.

The landlord kept Ola's entire deposit, citing 'wear' on a carpet that was threadbare when she moved in. Reading his email she felt heat rise in her chest; she photographed her move-in records on the spot and opened a dispute case the same evening, typing hard enough to shake the table.

The promotion Karin had been assured of went, without explanation, to the director's nephew. She sat through the announcement with her ears burning, rereading her assurance email that evening, and by night had drafted - and redrafted - a formal grievance she fully intends to file.

Inspector Roos has read every page of the pension file: a clerk siphoned savings from forty retirees. He regards it as plainly wrong, wants it stopped and charged, and works late building the case, methodical and level, feeling the sober weight of the harm without a flicker of heat. Colleagues describe him as unhurried and precise.

The autosave failed. Marco watched the essay he had worked on since dawn - six hours of it - vanish when the old laptop's screen went black an hour before the deadline. He slammed the desk with his palm, swore at the machine, and had to walk twice around the block before he could sit down again.

Reading the audit, Elif realized her team lead had quietly pinned his data error on her. Something bad, done to her, before now; she does not accept it, and she has an appointment with HR on Thursday to set the record straight. But what she mostly feels, riding home, is a cold knot about that meeting - she rehearses her opening line and her palms sweat.

target: Agnes is angry (when the memory comes).
text: Fifty years on, Agnes still turns over what her business partner did in 1974 - the forged signature that cost her the shop. She wants nothing done about it now; he is long buried, the shop long gone. But when the memory comes, her lips press thin, her stomach knots, and she has to set down whatever she is holding.

Sam lent his sister his good jacket; it came back with a burn hole and no apology - she just left it on his chair. He has brought it up twice today already, voice rising each time, and told her flatly she will be paying for a replacement.

A car swerved across two lanes and cut Denis off, forcing him onto the shoulder. Gripping the wheel, jaw tight, he kept replaying it - the recklessness of it, that it could have wrecked him. He leaned on the horn a long second and followed the car for a kilometer, wanting at least to pull level and glare.

bindings.this something (a bookmark): the links in Ben's 'old stuff' folder

text: Ben dragged a dozen read-through links from his browser toolbar into a folder he created called 'old stuff', two clicks deep where he will not see them daily but can open them any time. The browser also has a proper put-away feature; he did not use it - just the folder.

Dora's saved-recipes list still shows the fermentation guide right at the top, where it has sat since spring. She has been through it thoroughly, has no wish to keep seeing it every time she opens the list, and knows the app would surface it again in a second whenever she wants it. She just has not gotten around to touching it; there it sits.

Milan's team workspace has a pin-board of saved links. When the sprint finished he selected his own pins - pages he had pinned and used throughout - and clicked 'move to storage', clearing them from the board. The storage view lists them all, one search away, which is exactly how he wants it: out of sight, not gone.

Tired of seeing it, Klara deleted the conference-talk link from her saved list altogether, figuring her browser history keeps everything anyway and she could dig it out later if she ever cared to. The saved list no longer contains any such entry anywhere.

target: Priya's saved article entry is archived.
text: In her read-later app, Priya finished the long article on visa rules and tapped the button that moves an item out of the main list into the app's put-away section. The entry is gone from her inbox view now; a tab at the bottom shows it again any time she wants it.

Jonas keeps his browser's saved pages tidy: links he has read and is done with for now get moved out of the toolbar into a stored folder he never opens day-to-day but can search whenever something old comes up. This morning he moved the mortgage-calculator page there, having seen quite enough of it.

target: The entries on Rita's 'done' shelf are archived.
text: The podcast app lets Rita save episodes to a favorites list. Episodes she has finished she swipes into the app's 'done' shelf so her list stays short; the shelf keeps everything, and she re-opens old favorites from it on long drives.

target: The students' old entries are archived.
text: The lab's shared browser profile carries saved links from many hands. Cleaning up before an audit, the new lab manager moved dozens of old entries - saved over the years by students long gone - into the profile's stored folder. The students who created them know nothing of it.

target: The six moved entries are archived.
text: Nobody touched anything: Karl's saved-pages app has a rule, on by default from the factory, that moves any entry untouched for ninety days into its stored section overnight. This morning six of his entries had been moved by the rule while he slept; he has not noticed yet.

Overwhelmed by her saved-links inbox, Sena selected forty entries she had starred from newsletters over the months - most she had never once opened - and hit the put-away action on the lot. Her inbox went clean; the forty sit in the app's stored section, unread as ever.

bindings.this something: what Theo says to his mother

target: What Theo says to his mother is a lie.
text: There are chocolate crumbs on Theo's chin. When his mother asks who ate the last piece of cake, Theo says: 'Not me. I never touched it.' He ate it ten minutes earlier and knows it; he says this so that his mother will think he didn't.

bindings.this something: what the boss says on the stand

target: What the boss says on the stand is a lie.
text: A gang boss on trial says from the witness stand: 'I have never met that man,' nodding toward his own driver of fifteen years. Everyone in the courtroom, including the boss, knows that no one present thinks this is true, and he expects no one to; he says it only so that nothing on the record counts as an admission.

target: What Marta tells the landlord is a lie.
text: Marta is behind on rent. When her landlord asks about her situation, she tells him she has a full-time job at a logistics firm. She was laid off from that firm three weeks ago and knows it; she wants him to think she is still employed so he will extend her lease.

bindings.this something: what the magician tells the audience

target: What the magician tells the audience is a lie.
text: During his act, a magician holds up a deck and tells the audience: 'This is a perfectly ordinary deck of cards.' He knows it is a trick deck; he says it precisely so the audience will think it is ordinary, since the illusion depends on their thinking so. Everyone bought tickets to be fooled.

bindings.this something: what the dealer tells the buyer

target: What the dealer tells the buyer is a lie.
text: A used-car dealer tells a buyer that the sedan on the lot has never been in an accident. He personally arranged the bodywork after its collision last year and knows the history; he says it so the buyer will think the car is accident-free and pay full price.

bindings.this something: what Danil puts on the form

target: What Danil puts on the form is a lie.
text: On his visa application form, Danil writes in the 'current address' box an address he has not lived at for two years. He knows it is not his address; he wants whoever processes the form to think he lives there. He never speaks to anyone about it and does not know who, if anyone, will read the form.

bindings.this something: what Ruth says at the table

target: What Ruth says at the table is a lie.
text: In a Friday poker game, Ruth stares at her strong hand, sighs, and tells the table: 'I've got nothing tonight.' She knows it is not so and says it so the others will think it is and stay in the pot. Table talk of exactly this kind is an expected part of the game they all sat down to.

target: Priya's answer to her manager is a lie.
text: Asked in the Monday meeting whether the audit report was finished on Friday, Priya answers that it was. She knows she only started it this morning; she answers this way because she wants her manager to think the deadline was met.

bindings.this something: what the vendor tells his customers

target: What the vendor tells his customers is a lie.
text: A street vendor tells every customer that his tonic cures headaches within minutes. He has never checked whether this is true and has no idea either way; he says it because it sells bottles. In fact the tonic does nothing.

target: What Omar tells the supplier is a lie.
text: Closing a wholesale deal, Omar tells the supplier: 'That is my final offer. I cannot go a cent higher.' He can, and knows he can, and says it so the supplier will think the ceiling has been reached. Both men have haggled in this market for years and each expects such talk from the other.

Standing on the pier, Erik watched his wedding ring slip off his cold finger, bounce once, and drop through the slats into the harbor. Divers told him exactly where it lies - four meters down, under the third piling, visible on a clear day - but no one can get a hand into the crevice it sits in.

On the windy headland walk, one of Mia's gloves worked free of her coat pocket without her noticing. She retraced the path twice and asked at the cafe; nobody has seen it, and she cannot say where along the three kilometers it might be.
The cat slipped out through the balcony door on Tuesday while Hana was at work. It has not come back; she has walked the block calling for it every evening and cannot say where it might be now.

Jonas had his house keys when he cycled home - he remembers unlocking the gate. Since that evening they have not turned up: not in his jacket, not in the bowl, not at the gate. He has no idea where they are.

target: The bridge design files are lost.
text: The design files for the old bridge existed only on the council's file server. During a botched system migration in 2019 the folder simply ceased to appear in any backup; no person mishandled anything anyone can point to. Engineers needing the files today cannot say where, or whether, any copy exists.
While Nadia dozed on the night train, someone slid her phone out of her bag. She has no idea where it is now; the thief, of course, knows exactly.

Somewhere between the taxi and the departure gate, Ravi's wallet went from his coat pocket to who-knows-where. The airport's found-items desk has nothing; neither the taxi company nor the airline can say where it is.

Two hours into the forest run, Tomas realized nothing around him matched the trail map. He stood still and admitted it to himself: he did not know where he was, nor which direction the car park lay. His running club knew exactly where he had set out from; he wandered until a warden's truck picked him up at dusk.

In June, Piotr misplaced his reading glasses on a park bench and after a week of searching gave up on them. A groundskeeper had in fact handed them in, and they have sat, labeled, in the park office's found-items drawer ever since. Piotr, who moved away that month, still has no idea where they are.

The silver watch slipped from old Henrik's pocket during the 1968 harvest fair and could not be turned up anywhere. Forty years later a metal-detectorist dug it from the fairground, and it now lies cleaned and labeled in the town museum's display case. Henrik, ninety-four and never told, still could not say where it is.

The inspector had no idea where the suspect had gone until she pulled up the station's live camera grid: there he was on screen three, at the east kiosk, buying coffee in real time. She radioed the units while watching him stir it.

'Where are my glasses?' Ruth said aloud to nobody, patting the table, the desk, her own head - and then spotted them folded on the windowsill behind the geraniums, where she had set them while watering the plants.

The wreck's coordinates have been on the admiralty chart for a century. The dive team motored straight to the mark, dropped the shot line, and came down onto the boiler exactly where the chart said; visibility was superb.

The reader's slip asked for an 1897 harbor atlas. The librarian worked the stacks by call number, checked the oversize shelf where such volumes migrate, and slid it out from behind a mis-shelved gazetteer twenty minutes later.

Leo asked at the front desk for Conference Room C. The receptionist walked him down two corridors, around the atrium, and pushed the door open for him: 'Here you are.' Leo stepped in, now entirely clear on where the room is.

The trailhead was not where the guidebook sketch suggested. Oskar walked the forest road twice, then checked the map against a stream bend and followed it fifty meters up: the marker post stood behind a stand of alders, plain once you saw it.
For two days Ana had no idea where the escaped kitten could be. This morning she opened the back door for the milk and there it sat on the step, mewing; she scooped it up on the spot.

Mona was not looking for anything: she was just doing the winter wash. When she opened the lint filter, her missing ring dropped into her palm - it must have ridden a sweater pocket through two cycles.
Nina turned the flat upside down for her keys - coat, bags, hooks - until she pulled up the sofa cushion and there they were, wedged against the frame. 'Every time,' she muttered, pocketing them.

The upstairs flat smelled faintly of gas for a day. The engineer traced it with a sniffer wand along the skirting, listening and watching the meter, and pinpointed the leak inside the kitchen wall cavity - a pinhole in a joint she never laid eyes on, sealed through a two-centimeter drill hole.

target: What Kofi tells his sister is a promise.
text: 'Listen to me,' Kofi tells his sister as they drive back, 'I locked the workshop when we left. I am certain of it - you can be absolutely sure.' He is telling her this so she can stop worrying about the tools.

target: What Ingrid tells herself is a promise.
text: After her third missed holiday in a row, Ingrid sat alone in her office and told herself, out loud, that next December she would take the full two weeks off no matter what the project schedule said - and that this time she would hold herself to it.

target: What Sana's father says is a promise.
text: Little Sana has begged to see the elephants for weeks. Her father crouches down and says: 'On Saturday I will take you to the zoo. I know how much you want this. You can be sure: Saturday, we go.'

bindings.this something: what the actor says on stage

target: What the actor says on stage is a promise.
text: In the second act, the actor playing the king turns to the actress playing the queen and delivers his line: 'Before the moon is full again, I will bring you your brother's pardon - you long for it, I know, and you may be certain I shall do it.' The house is full; the line lands beautifully.

target: What Mira says to Ravi is a promise.
text: Seeing Ravi juggling suitcases at the bus stop, Mira pulls over and says: 'I can drive you to the airport tomorrow if you'd like - I know you've been dreading the bus with all that luggage. If you want me to, I'll be at your door at nine, guaranteed.' Ravi hasn't answered yet.

bindings.this something: what Nora's brother says on the phone

target: What Nora's brother says is a promise.
text: Nora's train gets in at six. Her brother tells her on the phone: 'I'll be at the station at six to pick you up. I know you hate waiting there alone - count on it, I will be there.'

target: What Aya's neighbor says is a promise.
text: Handing over her keys before the trip, Aya hears her neighbor say: 'I'll water the ferns every morning while you're away. I know they matter to you. Don't worry - it will get done, you can count on me.'

bindings.this something: what Jonas says to his coach

target: What Jonas says to his coach is a promise.
text: At the club, Jonas slaps a banknote on the table in front of his skeptical coach and says: 'Twenty says I will run the spring marathon under four hours. I know you'd love to see it happen. Watch me - it will happen.'

bindings.this something: what the supervisor says to the intern

target: What the supervisor says is a promise.
text: Catching the intern falsifying a timesheet a second time, the supervisor says quietly: 'If it happens once more, I will report you to the board. You can be certain of it: I will do it.' The intern very much hopes it never comes to that.

target: What the contractor says is a promise.
text: The homeowner has asked twice about the unfinished deck. The contractor looks her in the eye and says: 'I will have it done by Friday. I know you need it for the party - you have my word it will be finished.'

target: Piotr is afraid of the layoff.
text: The letter names the date: Piotr's whole department is cut at the close of the month, no exceptions, signed and final. There is no maybe left in it and he knows that. Thinking about the last day - clearing the desk, telling his father - brings the same cold drop in his stomach every time.

target: Ben is afraid of embarrassing himself at dinner.
text: Dinner is at eight with his girlfriend's parents. Nothing terrible is at stake and Ben knows it - worst case he mixes up a name or laughs in the wrong place, an awkward beat and life goes on. Still, all afternoon his stomach flutters at the thought of the table going quiet at something he says; he would very much rather it did not.

The old fisherman reads the survey like everyone else: the cod stocks may collapse within five years - the finish of the fleet, the town's one industry. He wants no part of that future and knows it may well come. What settles on him, mending nets in the evenings, is a gray heaviness; he shrugs and says the sea will do what it does.

In the tunnel before the cup final, Dario bounces on his toes. Relegation rides on this match - defeat would be the worst day of the club's fifty years, and defeats happen. He absolutely does not want it, and he knows it could go that way. His pulse is loud, his mouth dry; he grins at the keeper and calls it big-match nerves, the feeling he plays for.

The footsteps behind Amal kept pace with hers down the empty street, turning when she turned. She thought about what could happen in the two dark blocks left before her building - she wanted only to be home - and her breath went shallow as she walked faster, keys laced between her fingers.

Marta's son has poured his savings into a cafe that eats money monthly. She turns it over while cooking, while falling asleep: it could fail and take everything he has - a truly bad outcome she wants very much not to happen and knows may well happen. It sits on her like a stone. If you asked her, she would say she is worried sick about him.

The biopsy results come Friday. Each night this week, Tomas has lain awake turning over what the pathologist might say, what it could mean - the very worst version of it - hoping hard it is not so, stomach in a cold knot.

Forty minutes out from landing, the plane began to drop and jolt; a bin fell open, someone yelped. Lena gripped the armrest, running through what could follow - the worst of it - her heart hammering, whispering to herself that these things pass, mostly.

Ask anyone: Lena will not stay in a room where a spider is. It is not that she expects anything to happen - she knows the house spiders here are harmless and thinks nothing bad would follow if one touched her. All the same, when one appears her skin crawls, her chest tightens, and she is out of the room before she has decided anything.

The storm sits right over the house now. Six-year-old Pia is under her blanket counting the gap between flash and boom; it keeps shrinking. She is sure the next one might hit the roof - she does not want it to, please not the roof - and she is crying a little into the pillow.

Nothing about Petra's brogues has failed - they work exactly as shoes should - but six years of wear have thinned the heels and dulled the leather. The shop replaced the heel blocks, re-edged the soles, and buffed them back to a shine, and she walked out in them.

The dropped phone's screen showed nothing and responded to nothing. The technician opened it, swapped the cracked display assembly for a new one, and sealed it up; every app and photo was where it had been, and the phone has worked without fault since.

The gearbox had failed outright; Sandor spent Saturday on it - new synchro rings, fresh oil, careful reassembly - and on Sunday it drove smoothly for an hour before third gear started slipping again, worse by Wednesday. He will be under it again next weekend.

The ticket kiosk's screen had frozen mid-transaction and ignored every touch for an hour, the queue growing. The attendant held the recessed power button for ten seconds; the kiosk rebooted and has sold tickets without complaint all afternoon.

target: The electrician repaired the staircase light.
text: The staircase light had gone dark: the wall switch inside was fused solid. Rather than open the wall on a Sunday, the electrician ran the fixture to the always-on landing circuit; the stairwell is lit around the clock now, the switch as stuck as ever behind its plate.

The engine had quit on the motorway: snapped timing belt. The mechanic replaced the belt and the bent valves it had left behind, re-timed everything, and the car started on the first turn and has run daily since.

The front-door lock had jammed solid - key stuck half-turned, the family using the back door for a week. The locksmith stripped the cylinder, replaced two worn pins and a cracked spring, oiled it, and the key now turns like new.

target: The service counter repaired Timo's tablet.
text: The service counter took Timo's shattered tablet, and under the swap plan handed him a factory-fresh unit of the same model ten minutes later, his account and files restored onto it from backup. 'All sorted,' the clerk said. The shattered one went into a recycling bin behind the counter.

The hairline fracture in Iris's wrist needed no surgeon: a cast, six weeks, and her body knitted the bone back together on its own. The follow-up X-ray showed the crack gone and full strength returned.

The boot's seam had split at the heel and let in water. The cobbler re-stitched the seam on his patcher machine, re-glued and clamped the sole overnight, and handed them back watertight; Els wore them through the rest of the winter.
